information_for_contributors: This TextMate grammar is a SECOND, hand-maintained source of truth for NL++ syntax, parallel to grammar.js (the tree-sitter grammar). Change one, change the other.

tree-sitter drives precise editor/LSP highlighting; this drives the fast first-paint in VS Code before the language server loads, and highlighting on the web via Shiki (which consumes TextMate grammars natively).
scripts/check-highlight-parity.mjs enforces that the two agree on an enumerated set of tokens. Run `npm run test:parity` after touching either file.

Known-approximate and deliberately NOT enforced: the type-vs-name disambiguation (a leading `field string name` needs a GLR conflict to resolve) and custom-block keywords (any identifier can be one). tree-sitter/the LSP resolve these; a regex grammar can only guess. Parity fixtures avoid asserting on those.
name: NL++

prose-block.comment: /? ... ?/ reaches the LLM verbatim; opaque, so no inner patterns. Delimiters and text are all @string.special in the tree-sitter query.
prose-block.name: string.other.prose.nlpp

fill-in.comment: ??? plus the rest of the line. Marker and hint are both @string.special.

import.comment: The path that follows is a real string, highlighted by #strings.
import.name: keyword.control.import.nlpp

define.comment: define <name> <body-to-EOL>. The body is one opaque token in the grammar (quotes are NOT a string), so we consume the whole line and leave the body unscoped to match.

object-block.comment: layer/module/service/component/class/interface/enum/type <Name>. The keyword is always followed directly by the name — no optional type — so this is unambiguous. Any modifiers before it are consumed by #keywords first; inherits/implements clauses after the name are left to #keywords too.

function-block.comment: function/method/getter/setter [return-type] name. Region ends BEFORE the params '(' and body '{' so those are handled by the top-level machinery. Inside, the optional return type and the name are told apart by lookahead: a type is followed by more (another identifier, '[' or '&'); the name is the identifier just before '(', '{' or EOL.

patterns.comment: return type: identifier followed by more of the declaration
patterns.name: entity.name.type.nlpp

patterns.comment: the declared name: identifier just before '(', '{' or EOL
patterns.name: entity.name.function.nlpp

field.comment: [modifiers] field [type] name. Modifiers are consumed by #keywords first. Region runs to EOL/comment so the recursive #type-arguments can highlight the template WITHOUT the outer rule consuming it. type vs name told apart by the same lookahead trick as functions.

patterns.comment: field type: identifier followed by more of the declaration
patterns.name: entity.name.type.nlpp

patterns.comment: the field name: last identifier before EOL/comment
patterns.name: variable.other.member.nlpp

custom-block.comment: APPROXIMATE — see information_for_contributors. Any NON-reserved identifier can be a block keyword (a defined term), followed by the name. The negative lookahead keeps this off reserved-keyword lines (field/class/method/...), which the anchored '^[ \t]*' would otherwise outrank by starting a column earlier. We only fire when the second identifier is followed by '{' or EOL; single-word header clauses can still be mis-highlighted. Not enforced by the parity test.

param-clause.comment: ( [type] name , ... ). '(' only ever opens a param clause in NL++, so this is safe at top level. The parens and separating commas are left unscoped to match highlights.scm, which captures type-argument brackets but not param punctuation. Same type/name lookahead, bounded by ',' and ')'.

patterns.comment: param type: identifier followed by more (bracket, '&', or the name)
patterns.name: entity.name.type.nlpp

patterns.comment: param name: identifier just before ',' or ')'
patterns.name: variable.parameter.nlpp

type-arguments.comment: [ type-arg (, type-arg)* ] — recursive, so nested templates highlight to any depth. '[' only ever opens type arguments in NL++.

auto.comment: @keyword.type in the tree-sitter query, so a keyword here too.
auto.name: keyword.other.auto.nlpp
